content.plain_english: This week in Year 5 maths, your child is working on Shape. The learning focuses on: identify 3d shapes, including cubes and other cuboids, from 2d representations and draw given angles and measure them in degrees. These skills build directly on what they have already mastered and prepare them for the next steps in the programme of study.
content.in_school: Children handle, sort, and classify 2-D and 3-D shapes, drawing and describing their properties. They use protractors, mirrors, and squared paper to explore angles, symmetry, and position. Explaining what they notice in precise mathematical language is a key part of every lesson.
content.home_activity: Go on a shape hunt around the house. How many rectangles can you spot? Any cylinders in the kitchen? Count vertices and edges on a cereal box or tin. For angles, look for right angles (corners of doors) and ask whether other angles are acute or obtuse.
dinner_table_questions: Can you show me how to identify 3d shapes, including cubes and other cuboids, from 2d representations?
Was it easy or tricky to draw given angles and measure them in degrees? Why?
How would you explain today's maths to someone who has never seen it before?

key_vocabulary.definition: A flat shape with length and width but no thickness — e.g. triangle, circle.
A solid shape with length, width, and height — e.g. cube, sphere, cone.
When one half of a shape is a mirror image of the other.
An angle of exactly 90° — the same as the corner of a square.
Lines that meet at a right angle (90°).

example_questions: Find the missing angle in the triangle (angles: 65°, 48°, ?).
Name a quadrilateral with diagonals that bisect at right angles.
Measure this angle with a protractor.
content.times_table_tip: By Year 5 children should know all their times tables with speed and confidence. Ask quick-fire questions at dinner — mixing tables so they recall facts rather than just reciting sequences. Can they answer in under 3 seconds?
